introduction: Surgical Care are initiatives aimed at reducing or eliminating complications arising from the provision of surgery to patients that need surgical operations. This is generally referred to as perioperative care which consists of preoperative, intra-operative and postoperative care.

features: History of cough is indicative of Cardiopulmonary disorders such as chest infections, Bronchial asthma, chronic obstructive airways diseases, hypertension, heart failure etc.

Patient may have metabolic disorders like Diabetic Mellitus etc, Haematologic disorders like the haemoglobinopathies e.g., Sickle cell disease. Other patients may give history of allergy to drugs, talc, elastoplast, antiseptics, etc.
Drug history like Propranolol, diuretics, steroids and other hormonal agents; prednisolone, oral contraceptives; tricyclic antidepressants, which may interfere with healing process.
Social habits like cigarette smoking, alcohol use etc may have effects on anaesthetic outcome.
Previous anaesthetic experience, type and how long ago are important

investigations: Chest radiograph: especially for patients 60 years and above, and those with chest infection gives evidence of chest infection and cardiomegaly
ECG: for patients over 60 years and those with heart disease or hypertension
Pulmonary function tests may be necessary in patients with obstructive airways disease
Urine sugar to exclude diabetes mellitus in patients with history of polyuria, polydipsia and polyphagia
Haemogram and complete blood count in all patients
Haemoglobin genotype in patients with suspected haemoglobinopathies
Clotting profile (PT, KCCT) where there is suspicion of bleeding diathesis e.g., in jaundiced patients
Other investigations as may be indicated by individual clinical circumstances

goals: Ensure early identification of Complications and prompt treatment
Ensure quick recovery and return to activities
non_drug: Treat chest infections with appropriate antibiotics
Correct acidosis (by adequate rehydration in patients without renal impairment)
Correct anaemia by haematinics or blood transfusion when necessary
Ensure adequate hydration in patients with sickle cell anaemia to avoid sickling and sludging
Evaluate high-risk patients meticulously to ensure optimal fitness for surgery
Ensure appropriate positioning post-surgery for airway management and reduction of venous pressures
Close monitoring of cardiopulmonary status post-surgery
Chest physiotherapy to prevent basal atelectasis and hypostatic pneumonia
drug: Control diabetes with soluble insulin during long surgical procedures
Administer intramuscular vitamin K for suspected bleeding diathesis
Analgesics for pain management: use multimodal approach with local anaesthetics and opioids
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Avoid irrational or indiscriminate use of antibiotics
supportive_measures: Encourage mobilization and ambulation as early as practicable
Guide surgical team and peri-operative nursing team for intra-operative care based on WHO's 'Surgical Safety Checklist'
Close monitoring of all intake and output, as well as fluid and electrolyte balance
Parenteral nutrition if oral intake is prohibited for more than 48-72 hours
Monitor and manage all indwelling tubes, catheters and drains to avoid infection
Involvement of the surgeon in postoperative care to ensure continuity and avoid poor care
Use electronic monitors post major surgery where available

complications: Sickling crises in patients with sickle cell anaemia
Postoperative pyrexia due to malaria, atelectasis, hypostatic pneumonia, wound infection, urinary tract infection
